title: Kaitiakitanga o Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku | Guardianship of the Mangakōtukutuku Stream
subject: Science
culturalContext: Students will explore kaitiakitanga through the lens of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku, a significant stream flowing through our rohe. This awa holds deep cultural significance for Ngāti Kahungunu as a traditional food source and navigation route. The lesson connects to the pūrākau of Hinepūkohurangi, the mist maiden whose tears are said to feed the stream, emphasizing the interconnectedness of all living systems.
learningObjectives: Conduct and analyze water quality tests (pH, nitrate levels, turbidity) from three locations along Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku
Compare scientific water quality data with mātauranga Māori indicators of stream health (presence of native species, water clarity)
Create a kaitiakitanga action plan proposing specific, evidence-based interventions to improve the health of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku
Present findings using both scientific terminology and te reo Māori concepts of environmental stewardship

activities.title: Whakawhanaungatanga ki te Awa
activities.description: Students visit Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku with local kaumātua who share pūrākau and historical significance of the waterway. Students make observational sketches and record initial water quality observations using their senses (within safety guidelines).

activities.title: He Tātari Wai - Water Testing
activities.description: Using water testing kits, students collect and analyze water samples from three strategic points along the awa (upstream, near college, downstream). They measure pH, nitrate levels, turbidity, and temperature, recording data in field journals.

activities.title: Te Hōtaka Taiao - Environmental Mapping
activities.description: Students create GIS maps or detailed hand-drawn maps overlaying their scientific data with cultural observations (native plant presence, bird sightings, signs of pollution). They identify correlations between scientific measurements and ecosystem health.

activities.title: Te Whakaaro Kaitiaki - Guardian Thinking
activities.description: Working in small groups, students develop evidence-based action plans addressing one identified issue affecting the awa. Plans must integrate both scientific solutions (e.g., planting recommendations based on nitrate absorption rates) and cultural approaches (e.g., organizing community clean-up events).

resources: Local kaumātua from Ngāti Kahungunu with knowledge of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku
Water quality testing kits (pH strips, nitrate tests, turbidity tubes, thermometers)
NIWA's 'Stream Health Monitoring and Assessment Kit' (SHMAK) resources
Te Ara Encyclopedia entry 'Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku - Cultural Significance'

tasks: Submit a detailed scientific report analyzing water quality data from three stream locations
Create a visual presentation (poster or digital) comparing scientific findings with mātauranga Māori indicators of health
Present a 3-minute pechakucha proposing a specific kaitiakitanga action for the stream

nzcAlignment: Nature of Science: Investigating in Science - Students will carry out science investigations using a variety of approaches
Living World: Ecology - Explain how living things are suited to their particular habitat and how they respond to environmental changes
Planet Earth and Beyond: Earth Systems - Investigate the water cycle and its dynamic interactions with other earth systems
NZC Key Competencies: Participating and Contributing - Students will contribute to the cultural and environmental well-being of their stream
